---
title: "Statistical Tests"
author: "Yang Liu"
date: 02-16-2026
format: 
   revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    theme: simple
    incremental: true
    scrollable: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
editor: visual
---
## Core Curriculum  {#slide-1}

2-16-2026

## Proper data {#slide-2}

Valid

Reliable

Sufficient

## Valid data  {#slide-3}

Internal validity

Data is accurate and true

Study design effectively collects outcome data

External validity:  results apply to the broader population

a representative sample size

clear inclusion/exclusion criteria

multi-center trials

Validated instruments

New

adapted

## FREE Resource!  {#slide-4}

Assistance with survey and questionnaire design from the Institute for Social Research

Click here for more information and to submit a request

## Existing Instrument {#slide-5}

Pilot test

https://bookdown.org/alex_leith/mc451/selecting-and-adapting-research-scales.html

## Proper Analysis Example {#slide-6}

Scenario:  A randomized trial with 150 patients assigned equally to Drug A and Drug B, with clear inclusion/exclusion criteria, and standardized measurement of blood pressure.

Action:  Statistical comparison is suitable; sample size is reasonable, methods are valid.

## Improper Analysis Example {#slide-7}

Scenario:  Comparing outcomes from a handful of case reports (e.g., 2 with Drug A, 1 with Drug B), using a t-test.

Action:  This is inappropriate; data are insufficient and not systematically collected.

## Proper dataset format {#slide-8}

![](ppt/media/image1.png "Picture 3")

## Proper statistical methods {#slide-9}

Statistical methods should match 

data type (e.g., categorical, continuous) 

study design (e.g., descriptive, randomized trial, prediction modeling).

## Data type {#slide-10}

Categorical 

Continuous

## Slide 11

![](ppt/media/image2.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 12

![](ppt/media/image3.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 13

![](ppt/media/image4.png "Picture 3")

## Likert-type data: ordinal data {#slide-14}

![What is a Likert Scale? Guide & Examples \| Figma](ppt/media/image5.png "Picture 2")

## Proper Analysis Example {#slide-15}

Scenario:  Comparing mean cholesterol levels (continuous data) between two independent groups with an independent samples t-test.

Action:  Test matches data type and structure.

## Improper Analysis Example {#slide-16}

Scenario:  Using a chi-square test to compare means of cholesterol (continuous data).

Action:  Wrong test for the data type.

## Proper assumptions {#slide-17}

Many tests have assumptions: normality, independence, etc.

If assumptions aren't checked or aren't met, results can be misleading.

## Slide 18

![](ppt/media/image6.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 19

![When Should You Delete Outliers from a Data Set? - Atlan \| Humans of Data](ppt/media/image7.jpeg "Picture 4")

## Normality test {#slide-20}

Shapiro Wilk test

swilk  age

P\<0.05

Normal distribution is not assumed

Median(IQR)

P≥0.05

Normal distribution is assumed

Mean(SD)

## Mean vs median {#slide-21}

Both measure central tendency

Median: reduce noise

Example:

Age 20, 30, 35, 36, 38, 39, 80

Mean: 39.7

Median: 36

## Statistical test {#slide-22}

Independent data

Median(IQR)

Wilcoxon rank sum test

Mean(SD)

Independent samples t-test

## Slide 23

  Variables        Normal distribution   
  ---------------- --------------------- ------------
  Age              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  BMI              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  Length of stay   Yes                   Mean( sd )

## Slide 24

  Variables        Normal distribution   
  ---------------- --------------------- -------------
  Age              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  BMI              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  Length of stay   No                    Median(IQR)

## Slide 25

  Variables        Normal distribution   
  ---------------- --------------------- -------------
  Age              Yes                   Median(IQR)
  BMI              Yes                   Median(IQR)
  Length of stay   No                    Median(IQR)

## Proper Analysis Example {#slide-26}

Scenario:  Authors perform a Shapiro-Wilk test for normality before using a t-test. If assumptions fail, they use the Wilcoxon rank sum test.

Action:  Appropriate checking and adaptation to statistical requirements.

## Improper Analysis Example {#slide-27}

Scenario:  Authors blindly use t-tests on strongly skewed data without checking normality, ignoring outliers.

Action:  Results may not be valid or generalizable.

## True significant difference {#slide-28}

Significant differences may arise if groups differ in baseline characteristics ("confounding"). It's crucial to examine and adjust for comparability.

## Proper Analysis Example {#slide-29}

Scenario:  Before comparing treatment outcomes, authors present a table of baseline variables (age, sex, comorbidities) and use regression or propensity score methods to adjust for differences.

Action:  Ensures groups are comparable---reducing bias.

## Improper Analysis Example {#slide-30}

Scenario:  Authors report Drug A is better, but the group receiving Drug A is 10 years younger and healthier (no adjustment).

Action:  Significant difference likely due to group differences, not treatment effect.

## Real-life example {#slide-31}

Uncontrolled Demographic Factors :  The MRFIT study, which found low cholesterol was associated with higher death rates, but failed to control for heavy alcohol consumption, which caused both low cholesterol and accidental deaths.

## Proper use of statistical techniques {#slide-32}

Incorrect technique choice or execution---e.g., failure to account for small sample size or paired data.

## Slide 33

  Variables           Hypertension Yes n=79   Hypertension No n=46
  ------------------- ----------------------- ----------------------
  BMI                                         
     Under weight     2 (2.5%)                1 (2.2%)
     Normal           66 (83.5%)              27 (58.7%)
     Obesity          8 (10.1%)               12 (26.1%)
     Morbid obesity   3 (3.8%)                6 (13.0%)

37.5% cells have a count less than 5

## Chi-square test VS Fisher's exact test {#slide-34}

\<20% 

Chi-square test or Fisher's exact test

\>= 20% and \<= 50%

Fisher's exact test

\>50%

Neither

## Slide 35

![](ppt/media/image8.png "Picture 4")

![Paired T-Test - Definition, Formula \...](ppt/media/image9.png "Picture 4")

![](ppt/media/image10.png "Picture 5")

## Statistical test {#slide-36}

Paired continuous data

Median(IQR)

Wilcoxon signed rank test

Mean(SD)

paired t-test

## Statistical test {#slide-37}

Paired categorical data

n(%)

McNemar's test

## Likert-type data {#slide-38}

Descriptive statistics for individual item

\*\*\*C ategorical, number (percentage)

\*\* " C ontinuous", median 

ranked categories rather than precise, equal intervals.

\* " C ontinuous", mean, not recommended 

## Slide 39

![](ppt/media/image11.png "Picture 4")

## Slide 40

![](ppt/media/image12.png "Picture 4")

## Slide 41

![](ppt/media/image13.png "Picture 4")

https://www.mdpi.com/2673-8392/5/1/18

## Proper Analysis Example {#slide-42}

Scenario:  Authors use paired analyses for before-and-after measurements.

Action:  Matches test to design.

## Improper Analysis Example {#slide-43}

Scenario:  Authors use an unpaired t-test on paired data.

Action:  Techniques don't match data.

## Properly report the type of test used/ significance level {#slide-44}

A transparent report names statistical tests used and defines the significance threshold (e.g., p\<0.05), so readers interpret results confidently.

## Proper Analysis Example {#slide-45}

Scenario:  "We used the Wilcoxon rank sum test to compare median survival, with significance set at p\<0.05."

Action:  Complete and clear, readers know the methods.

## Improper Analysis Example {#slide-46}

Scenario:  "Survival was compared and found significant"---no test or threshold named.

Action:  Lack of clarity; results are hard to interpret or reproduce.

## Report sensitivity and  specificity {#slide-47}

Diagnostic performance requires both sensitivity (true positive rate) and specificity (true negative rate). Reporting only one is incomplete and potentially misleading.

## Proper Analysis Example {#slide-48}

Scenario:  Authors report a new blood test has sensitivity 90% and specificity 85% for detecting heart attack.

Action:  Both metrics included for full diagnostic value.

## Improper Analysis Example {#slide-49}

Scenario:  "Our test caught 90% of heart attacks" (sensitivity), but specificity (false positive rate) is not reported.

Action:  Without specificity, risk of many false positives is unknown.

## Report missing data {#slide-50}

## Exercise {#slide-51}

## 1. Comparing Mean Blood Pressure Between Two Groups {#slide-52}

Scenario:  You want to compare mean systolic blood pressure between patients treated with Drug A vs. Drug B.

Test:  

A. Independent samples t-test 

B. Wilcoxon rank sum test 

## 2. Change in Pain Score Pre- vs. Post-Treatment {#slide-53}

Scenario:  Measuring pain scores (0--10) in the same patients before and after physical therapy.

Test:  

A. Paired t-test

B. Wilcoxon signed rank test 

## 3. Comparing Rate of Post-op Infection in Surgery Types {#slide-54}

Scenario:  Comparing infection rates (yes/no) after two types of surgical procedures.

Test:  

A. Chi-square test for independence or Fisher's exact test 

B. Wilcoxon rank sum test 

## 4. Association Between Smoking (Y/N) and COPD (Y/N) {#slide-55}

Scenario:  Examining the association between smoking status and COPD diagnosis in a cohort.

Test:  

A. Chi-square test for independence 

B. Fisher's exact test 

## 5. Correlation Between LDL and Triglycerides {#slide-56}

Scenario:  You hypothesize that higher LDL cholesterol is correlated with higher triglyceride levels. 

LDL cholesterol: continuous, normal distribution

Triglyceride: continuous, not normal distribution

Test:  

A. Pearson correlation 

B. Spearman rank correlation 

## 6. Comparing Median Survival Times {#slide-57}

Scenario:  Do two cancer treatment groups have different median survival times?

Test:  

A. Wilcoxon rank sum test 

B. Wilcoxon signed rank test

## 7. Prevalence of Complications Before and After Intervention {#slide-58}

Scenario:  Did a new protocol reduce the number of patients experiencing a complication (before/after intervention) in paired data?

Test:  

A. McNemar's test

B. Chi-square test

## 8. Generic Drug Compared to Brand {#slide-59}

Scenario:  Is a generic drug "not worse" than the brand in controlling blood glucose (HbA1c levels)?

Test:  

A.  Non-inferiority test (a one-sided t-test with pre-specified margin).

B. Independent samples t-test

## 9. Comparing clinical outcomes  {#slide-60}

Scenario:  You want to compare clinical outcomes across two groups in an observational study, adjusting for baseline characteristics. Case group n=48

   Control group n=523

Method:  

A. Propensity score matching

B.  Nothing

## 10. Assessing Effect of Three Therapies on Depression Scores {#slide-61}

Scenario:  Three groups receive different psychotherapies, and you compare their post-treatment depression scores.

Test:  

A. ANOVA (if data are normal); Kruskal-Wallis test (non-parametric).

B. Independent samples t-test or Wilcoxon rank sum test

## 11. Assessing Relationship Between Pain Improvement and Age  {#slide-62}

Scenario:  Does greater age correlate with pain improvement (ranked outcome, not normally distributed)?

Test:  

A. Spearman rank correlation

B. Pearson correlation

## 12. Comparing Proportions With Very Small Sample Sizes {#slide-63}

Scenario:  In a pilot study, you want to know if the rate of rare side effects differs between two drugs, with low event counts.

Test:  

A. Fisher's exact test

B. Chi-square test

## Analysis of the results (1-5) {#slide-64}

Were the data worthy of statistical analysis? If so, were the methods of analysis appropriate to the source and nature of the data? 	

Were the appropriate assumptions met for the statistical methods utilized? 	

Were there sufficient analyses to ascertain whether "significant difference" might, in fact, have been due to a lack of comparability of the groups: (i.e. age, sex, clinical characteristics etc.) ? 	

Was there an improper use of statistical techniques? 	

Was there mention of the type of test used or the significance level? 	

Was there use of measured sensitivity without specificity?	

## Slide 65

![](ppt/media/image14.jpeg "Online Media 3")

## Slide 66

![](ppt/media/image15.jpeg "Online Media 3")
